bd_articles.title: $9M Country Club sale tops February home transactions
bd_articles.author: Sara B. Hansen
bd_articles.summary: Castle Rock and Wash Park mansions rounded out the top 5.
bd_articles.content_preview:
181 Race St. (Photos courtesy of REColorado)
A renovated and expanded 1927 Denver Country Club mansion sold for its $9 million asking price, making it February’s highest priced metro home sale.
Other standout luxury sales last month included a Castle Rock equestrian estate, a newly built home near Washington Park and two mansions in Cherry Hills Village.
When the Blake O’Shaughnessy 2006 Trust purchased 181 Race St. for $3.8 million in January 2020, the 7,500-square-foot home had already been gu

bd_articles.title: Hopes of revitalization for a derelict Thornton shopping center: ‘We need to have a gathering space’
bd_articles.author: John Aguilar
bd_articles.summary: The city plans to remove contaminated soils, then find developer to breathe new life into site.
bd_articles.content_preview:
An aerial view of the former Thornton Shopping Center in Thornton on Feb. 25, 2026. (Hyoung Chang/The Denver Post)
Thornton is staring 1955 in the face and saying, “No thanks.”
That was the year the Thornton Shopping Center first sprouted on a 15-acre parcel at the corner of East 88th Avenue and Washington Street. It was a marvel of post-war suburban America — complete with a Woolworth’s and Miller’s Market.
But time took its toll, rendering the strip mall a crumbling, blighted mess as the city

bd_articles.title: Farbers sell LoHi apartment development site for $9.5M
bd_articles.author: Thomas Gounley
bd_articles.summary: The buyer said a groundbreaking is imminent.
bd_articles.content_preview:
The lot at 3301-3333 Mariposa St. in Denver on March 5, 2026. (Thomas Gounley/BusinessDen)
The Farber brothers have sold a site in LoHi with approved development plans, and a groundbreaking is imminent.
Elevation Development Group, founded by Brent, Brad and Gregg Farber, sold 0.68 acre at 3301-3333 Mariposa St. in Denver on Wednesday to Greenwood Village-based Century Communities.
Century, a large homebuilder that also develops apartments, paid $9.5 million, records show. That works out to $322

bd_articles.title: Five takeaways from Denver’s restaurant report
bd_articles.author: Max Scheinblum
bd_articles.summary: Costs are up while employment and profit are down.
bd_articles.content_preview:
Marlee Brown serves guests at Trybal African Speakeasy in Denver on Feb. 25, 2026. (Kevin Mohatt/Special to The Denver Post)
Denver’s restaurant scene is in crisis.
So much so that the city, VisitDenver and Austin, Texas-based restaurant financing company InKind commissioned a report to detail the industry.
Denver’s rising tipped minimum wage, which has more than doubled since 2019 and sits at $16.27 an hour, was the biggest complaint of local restaurateurs. But the 67-page document outlined a h

bd_articles.title: The Docket: Real estate lawsuit roundup for 3.5.26
bd_articles.author: Justin Wingerter
bd_articles.summary: Foreclosure at a kosher deli that owes $2.1 million, a real estate investment firm splits, a lender wants Athmar Park apartments, and more.
bd_articles.content_preview:
The Alfred A. Arraj U.S. Courthouse at 901 19th St. in downtown Denver. (BusinessDen file)
Adams District Court
SK University Hills Owner MZL LLC v. C&M Landscaping Services LLC; Maira Rodriguez
The plaintiff landlord claims it inadvertently sent $112,000 in checks to the defendants, who cashed them despite having never spoken to or done work for the plaintiff.
Attorneys: Amanda H. Halstead, Mills Halstead & Zaloudek
Filed: 2/25/2026
26cv30417
Eric Slovacek; Audrey Slovacek v. State Farm Fire an

bd_articles.title: Colorado Coalition for the Homeless to develop DHA site along Federal
bd_articles.author: Thomas Gounley
bd_articles.summary: The Denver Housing Authority paid $10.95 million in 2020 for the site just south of I-70, once home to a Rodeway Inn.
bd_articles.content_preview:
The 4745 Federal Blvd. property on Feb. 25, 2026. The building to the rear is on a separate lot. (Thomas Gounley/BusinessDen)
The Denver Housing Authority has tapped the Colorado Coalition for the Homeless to develop a site along Federal Boulevard just south of Interstate 70.
CCH will develop up to 135 units of income-restricted housing on 3.8 acres at 4745 Federal Blvd., which is on the edge of the Berkeley and Sunnyside neighborhoods.
The property was formerly home to a Rodeway Inn motel, whic

bd_articles.title: Wash Park ice cream shop closes after a year
bd_articles.author: Max Scheinblum
bd_articles.summary: Colfax and Cream shuttered its other two locations last year.
bd_articles.content_preview:
Colfax and Cream’s doors had curtains in them when a BusinessDen reporter visited the store March 3. (Max Scheinblum/BusinessDen)
Colfax and Cream had a rocky road.
In recent weeks, the ice cream and soda shop closed its Wash Park location at 712½ S. Pearl St. in Denver. The spot opened at the corner of Pearl and Exposition Avenue on March 3 last year, according to an Instagram post from owner Tullie Bailey.
Bailey and his wife and co-owner, Senait Eritrea, did not respond to requests for commen

bd_articles.title: Construction firm owner ‘captive in his own company,’ asks judge to close it
bd_articles.author: Justin Wingerter
bd_articles.summary: “Our clients categorically dispute the claims,” says a lawyer for Cubex Restoration.
bd_articles.content_preview:
Cubex is a Denver-based home restoration company. (Facebook)
The reported unraveling of a construction firm includes details more befitting of Hollywood than Denver, like a private eye surveilling a co-owner and threats of a Honduran hitman.
Jared Gross, who is one-third of Cubex Restoration, a Denver remodeler founded two years ago, sued his business partners Feb. 20. He wants a judge to shut down the firm.
“The plaintiff has become captive in his own company,” according to Gross’ lawsuit.
The

bd_articles.title: Duplexes eyed for Shops at Northfield site where apartments were proposed
bd_articles.author: Matt Geiger
bd_articles.summary: The change could drop the unit count on 15 acres from 1,500 to 210.
bd_articles.content_preview:
The entrance to 48th Street within The Shops at Northfield in northeastern Denver. (Courtesy Stockdale Capital Partners)
The Shops at Northfield may be dropping apartments in favor of duplexes.
On Wednesday, Arizona-based homebuilder BB Living submitted plans to Denver proposing 105 duplexes on a 15.5-acre site east of the open-air shopping center in Denver’s Central Park neighborhood.
It’s the same site where mall owner Stockdale previously proposed constructing between 1,000 and 1,500 apartmen

bd_articles.title: Ramos Law buys southeast Denver office for $3M
bd_articles.author: Matt Geiger
bd_articles.summary: “Our business is just really booming down south,” Ramos said.
bd_articles.content_preview:
The building was most recently a bank branch. (Courtesy of Ramos Law)
Attorney Joe Ramos doesn’t have a downtown corner office.
But in southeast Denver, he’ll have an office on a corner.
The head of personal injury firm Ramos Law purchased a 15,600-square-foot building at the corner of Hampden Avenue and Monaco Street in Denver last week for $3 million from UMB Bank. The deal works out to $189 a square foot.
Ramos Law will consolidate offices in east Denver and Centennial, both leased, into the

bd_articles.title: Hotel, credit union next to Central Park homeless shelter find a buyer
bd_articles.author: Thomas Gounley
bd_articles.summary: “It’s really affected all the properties in the vicinity,” a broker said of the shelter.
bd_articles.content_preview:
The former Courtyard by Marriott hotel at 7415 E. 41st Ave. in Denver on March 3, 2026. (Thomas Gounley/BusinessDen)
A credit union and hotel that flank a homeless shelter at the edge of Denver’s Central Park neighborhood have sold to the same buyer.
Aurora-based 2nd Home Community, a provider of adult day services and home care, purchased the former Partner Colorado Credit Union building at 4000 Quebec St. in January for $800,000, according to public records.
And on Friday, the company bought t

bd_articles.title: Former Punch Bowl Social exec succeeds Padró as CEO of restaurant group
bd_articles.author: Max Scheinblum
bd_articles.summary: The announcement comes after settlement of a lawsuit against Culinary Creative Group, which plans to pursue a “clustered growth” strategy.
bd_articles.content_preview:
Culinary Creative Group co-founder Juan Padro makes an announcement about food during the 9th annual annual orphans’ Christmas Wednesday, Dec. 25, 2019 at Highland Tap & Burger. The restaurant group announced Padro would step down as CEO of the company Wednesday, Feb. 25, 2026. (Daniel Brenner/Denver Post)
After 15 years at the helm of Culinary Creative Group, Juan Padró is leaving the kitchen.
“I’m a kid from Worcester. I’m an entrepreneur,” the Massachusetts native said. “I’m not this strategi

bd_articles.title: Modular builder Fading West demands millions from developer of Granby project
bd_articles.author: Justin Wingerter
bd_articles.summary: Fading West was told by Summit Housing to stop work on a $10 million order in October.
bd_articles.content_preview:
Fading West’s 110,000-square-foot manufacturing facility in Buena Vista. (Colorado Housing and Finance Authority)
A state-backed manufacturer of modular homes is seeking $5.7 million from a developer after that developer ordered it to stop work on a modular apartment project in Granby.
Fading West, which has been making modular buildings in Buena Vista since 2016, is asking an arbitrator to make Summit Housing Group, a Montana-based developer, pay up. Modular homes are created in a warehouse and

bd_articles.title: Fentress sells Broadway buildings to Magnetic for $13M
bd_articles.author: Thomas Gounley
bd_articles.summary: The architect behind DIA provided seller financing. The deal was once slated to be much bigger.
bd_articles.content_preview:
The Fentress Studios building at 421 Broadway in Denver on March 2, 2026. (Thomas Gounley/BusinessDen)
Architect Curtis Fentress has sold two buildings along Broadway in Denver, including one where his firm has long operated.
The founder of Fentress Architects, which was bought by Populous last year and is now known as Fentress Studios, sold 421 and 433 Broadway on Friday for $12.75 million, according to public records. The deal works out to $253 a square foot for the 1.15-acre site.
Curtis Fent

bd_articles.title: LoHi hotel being demolished ahead of apartment project
bd_articles.author: Thomas Gounley
bd_articles.summary: The former Residence Inn property along Zuni sold in December.
bd_articles.content_preview:
American Demolition has begun knocking down the former Residence Inn in LoHi. (Courtesy American Demolition)
A sprawling LoHi hotel is coming down.
Crews from American Demolition are knocking down the former Residence Inn at 2777 Zuni St. in Denver. 
The hotel, made up of a host of two-story buildings across 4 acres, was purchased in December by San Antonio, Texas-based Kairoi Residential. The company paid $39 million, or $231 a square foot for the land.
Development plans show Kairoi plans to bu

bd_articles.title: Former Avs defenseman launches beer brand in Denver
bd_articles.author: Max Scheinblum
bd_articles.summary: Tyson Barrie’s Chilly Ones are a 3% light lager and a soon-to-debut nonalcoholic version, plus a 4.5% to 5% option that's brewing.
bd_articles.content_preview:
From left: Chilly Ones co-founder and “Chief Chilly One” Tyson Barrie, brand ambassador and Avs star Nathan MacKinnon, COO Kimberley Kainth and CEO Matthew Clayton. (Courtesy Chilly Ones)
While most people know beers as “cold ones,” Tyson Barrie opts for a different name.
“We’ve always just called beers chilly ones,” the former Colorado Avalanche defenseman said.
Now, Barrie hopes his moniker goes mainstream with his beer brand Chilly Ones, which made its U.S. debut weeks ago in Colorado. He pla

bd_articles.title: Luzzatto clears ground lease hurdle for $300M downtown project
bd_articles.author: Matt Geiger
bd_articles.summary: His planned conversion of 633 and 621 17th St. had been hamstrung by a tiny ground lease under one of the buildings.
bd_articles.content_preview:
The office towers at 621 17th St., right, and 633 17th St. as seen from the corner of 17th and California streets on Nov. 19, 2024. (BusinessDen file)
Asher Luzzatto’s downtown office-to-residential conversion has new life after six months in purgatory.
“There were definitely a lot of moments where I wasn’t sure it was going to happen,” said Luzzatto, 38. 
On Friday, Luzzatto and Rhoda Krasner agreed to extend a ground lease expiration from 29 to 99 years at 633 17th St. The length of the lease

bd_articles.title: Chicago firm mismanaged project before abandoning it, RiNo developer says
bd_articles.author: Justin Wingerter
bd_articles.summary: “John Buck Co.’s spending bore no resemblance to the project budget,” Menalto says, citing $10 million in spending without a groundbreaking.
bd_articles.content_preview:
A rendering of the first phase of Hurley Place, to be constructed between the former Blue Moon Brewing and Ironton Distillery buildings. (Courtesy Menalto Development)
The RiNo firm Menalto Development is accusing a Chicago company of burning through more than $10 million “before putting a single shovel in the ground” at their project along Delgany Street, then stopping work and ceasing communication about it.
“Menalto is entitled to recover damages the John Buck Co. has caused Menalto and the f

bd_articles.title: Restaurant industry report calls for dropping Denver tipped wage to state minimum
bd_articles.author: Max Scheinblum
bd_articles.summary: Councilman Darrell Watson said Friday he plans to propose changes, although not that drastic.
bd_articles.content_preview:
Alexis Apodaca Fresquez brings coffee to tables for refills during the breakfast rush at Sam’s No. 3 diner in downtown Denver on Aug. 28, 2024. (RJ Sangosti/The Denver Post)
Increased labor costs are ​​“the most frequently cited challenge” among restaurant owners and the “most destabilizing factor” for Denver’s full-service establishments.
That’s according to a 67-page industry-led report released Friday, in which veteran Colorado restaurateurs Dana Faulk Query and Adam Schlegel detailed the cit

bd_articles.title: Golf-centric coworking business adding second location
bd_articles.author: Matt Geiger
bd_articles.summary: Within the first 13 months of opening, Josh Kaplan and Joe Skovgaard have reached 100% occupancy in Greenwood Village.
bd_articles.content_preview:
Joe Skovgaard, left, launched Clubhouse Work & Golf with Josh Kaplan in late 2024. (Matt Geiger/BusinessDen)
Josh Kaplan and Joe Skovgaard have set a tee time for an office building on Colorado Boulevard come late summer. 
The two entrepreneurs are opening a second location of Clubhouse Work & Golf, a coworking space with golf simulators, in 24,000 square feet at 720 S. Colorado Blvd. Most of that will be offices on the top floor of the building’s north tower, with space for five Trackman golf s

bd_articles.title: After a rocky ownership run, Crescent looks to sell the Brown Palace
bd_articles.author: Thomas Gounley
bd_articles.summary: “Our goal is to find a good buyer who is well capitalized, who appreciates the legacy and wants to take it to the next level.”
bd_articles.content_preview:
People pass by empty windows at the iconic Brown Palace Hotel in Denver on Sept. 4, 2024. (Helen H. Richardson/The Denver Post)
The Brown Palace Hotel, one of downtown’s most iconic buildings, is on the market.
Newmark has been tapped to market the 9-story, 243-room hotel at 321 17th St., which has been owned since 2018 by Crescent Real Estate. The Brown Palace opened in August 1892, making it Denver’s second-oldest hotel still in operation, behind the Oxford Hotel in LoDo.
A deal would also inc

daily.text:
• **Denver Post** cited 7 BD stories: Fentress Broadway sale, coworking golf expansion, Luzzatto conversion project, ex-Avs defenseman beer launch, Ramos Law office purchase, Fading West-Granby dispute, dumpling bracket

• **Brown Palace sale getting major pickup** — Denver Post, Denverite, 9News, Denver Gazette all covering BD's story on historic hotel listing

• **Hoodline** aggregated 3 BD stories: Fentress Broadway sale, Lowry sewer issues, Colorado lender consolidation

• **Social media amplification** — Multiple Instagram and Facebook posts citing BD's Brown Palace and Avalanche bourbon stories

• **9News, Denver Gazette** picked up Brown Palace hotel sale story

• **Denverite** covered Brown Palace sale with "Want to buy?" angle

• **Reddit Denver** shared BD story on Rocky Mountain High topic

• No reporter bylines identifiable in third-party coverage

• The Denver Post covered construction firm owner seeking court-ordered closure, describing himself as 'captive in his own company'

• The Denver Post reported on duplex development plans for Shops at Northfield site previously slated for apartments

• Facebook post references "Denver co. selling business for $1.5B cash" story but provides no additional context or commentary

• The Denver Post includes BusinessDen content on page 83 of court section, though specific story details not provided in mention

• primeravivienda.com published a lengthy, garbled headline about protein meal prep and keto delivery services in Pune, India that appears to be spam or auto-generated content with no clear connection to Denver business coverage

byline.text:
## Reporter Pickup Analysis - March 5, 2026

**Justin Wingerter** achieved a 50% pickup rate (1 of 2 articles) with his construction industry coverage resonating beyond BusinessDen's readership. His piece on the Fading West-Granby developer dispute caught The Denver Post's attention, suggesting that construction litigation and project disputes generate external interest. This single pickup indicates Wingerter's construction beat has crossover appeal, particularly when covering high-stakes financial conflicts in the development sector.

**Matt Geiger** delivered exceptional pickup performance with 100% of his articles (3 of 3) earning mentions from The Denver Post. His coverage spans commercial real estate transactions, major downtown development projects, and unique business concepts like golf-centric coworking spaces. The consistent pickup rate suggests Geiger's commercial real estate beat generates reliable external interest, with his ability to identify newsworthy angles across diverse property types—from law firm purchases to large-scale downtown developments—proving particularly valuable for broader media coverage.

**Max Scheinblum** had a 33% pickup rate (1 of 3 articles) with his sports-business crossover story gaining traction. The Denver Post's interest in his former Avalanche player beer brand launch demonstrates that celebrity-athlete business ventures generate external media interest. This pickup pattern suggests sports figures entering Denver's business landscape create natural crossover appeal for general news outlets covering both business and sports beats.

**Thomas Gounley** achieved a 50% pickup rate (2 of 4 articles) but generated the highest overall engagement with 7 total mentions. His Brown Palace sale story dominated pickup activity, earning coverage from major outlets including 9News, Denverite, and Denver Gazette, indicating that iconic Denver real estate stories have significant news value. The Fentress-Magnetic transaction also drew attention from both Hoodline and The Denver Post, suggesting Gounley's high-value commercial real estate coverage consistently generates broader media interest, particularly when involving landmark properties or substantial dollar amounts.

## Justin Wingerter
Wingerter achieved a 50% pickup rate (2 of 4 articles), with both successful pieces focusing on construction industry legal disputes. His story about the construction firm owner seeking judicial closure of his own company generated the strongest traction, earning coverage from both The Denver Post and Hoodline. The modular builder conflict over the Granby project also caught The Denver Post's attention, suggesting outlets find value in Wingerter's coverage of construction sector litigation and business disputes.

The pattern emerging from Wingerter's pickups indicates that stories combining legal drama with construction industry dysfunction resonate particularly well with other media outlets. Both picked-up pieces involved companies in distress or conflict, suggesting this niche of troubled construction businesses provides compelling content that other newsrooms want to amplify.

## Matt Geiger
Geiger achieved perfect pickup performance with all 4 articles gaining external coverage, though exclusively from The Denver Post. His stories span commercial real estate development and office sales, with the $300M Luzzatto downtown project representing the highest-value deal covered. The range includes everything from large-scale residential development (duplexes at Northfield) to niche business concepts (golf-centric coworking), demonstrating broad appeal across real estate sectors.

The consistent Post pickup rate suggests Geiger has established a reliable pipeline for commercial real estate news that complements rather than competes with the Post's coverage. His beat appears to generate steady, if not spectacular, external interest across diverse property types and deal sizes, indicating strong sourcing and news judgment in the commercial real estate space.

## Max Scheinblum
Scheinblum had a 25% pickup rate (1 of 4 articles), with his former Avalanche defenseman beer brand launch story gaining attention from The Denver Post. This represents his only external pickup during the period, suggesting his coverage areas may be more specialized or that his other stories didn't align with what other outlets were seeking.

The single pickup focusing on a sports celebrity's business venture indicates that stories combining recognizable personalities with local business launches have appeal beyond BusinessDen's readership, though Scheinblum's other coverage during this period didn't generate similar external interest.

## Thomas Gounley
Gounley posted the strongest pickup performance with 3 of 5 articles gaining external coverage, generating 10 total mentions across multiple outlets. His Brown Palace sale story dominated with 6 pickups spanning television (9News), print (Denver Gazette), and social media platforms, while his other real estate stories earned coverage from The Denver Post and Hoodline. The hotel/credit union sale near the Central Park homeless shelter also gained Facebook traction, suggesting social media interest in stories with community impact angles.

Gounley's success pattern reveals that high-value, iconic real estate transactions generate the most widespread coverage, particularly when involving Denver landmarks like the Brown Palace. His beat consistently produces content that other outlets find valuable, with a clear hierarchy where prestigious property sales vastly outperform routine commercial transactions in terms of external pickup and reach.

## Justin Wingerter
Wingerter achieved a 50% pickup rate with 2 of his 4 articles generating external interest. His construction company closure story performed best with coverage from both The Denver Post and Hoodline, while his Fading West legal dispute piece caught The Denver Post's attention. Both stories involved legal proceedings and business distress, suggesting Wingerter's legal/business conflict reporting resonates with other outlets. The pattern indicates that stories combining legal drama with business operations tend to generate the most external traction for his work.

## Matt Geiger
Geiger posted a perfect 100% pickup rate with all 4 articles receiving external coverage, primarily from The Denver Post. His real estate beat generated consistent interest across different property types and price points, from the $3M Ramos Law office purchase (which also gained social media traction on X.com) to development projects like the Northfield duplexes and Luzzatto's $300M downtown project. The consistent pickup pattern demonstrates that Denver's commercial real estate market is a reliable source of external interest, with The Denver Post serving as the primary aggregator of his real estate coverage.

## Max Scheinblum
Scheinblum achieved a 60% pickup rate with 3 of 5 articles picked up by other outlets. His coverage spans lifestyle and business leadership stories, with The Denver Post picking up both his Wash Park ice cream shop closure and former Avalanche player's beer brand launch. The restaurant industry executive appointment story gained traction on X.com, indicating social media interest in executive moves. His beat appears to focus on consumer-facing businesses and executive transitions, with local interest stories performing particularly well.

## Thomas Gounley
Gounley delivered a 67% pickup rate with 4 of 6 articles receiving external coverage, including his standout Brown Palace sale story that generated 6 mentions across major Denver outlets including 9News, Denverite, and Denver Gazette. His real estate and development beat consistently attracts attention, particularly stories involving high-profile properties or social issues like homelessness. The $13M Fentress Broadway sale and Central Park homeless shelter area hotel sale both received multi-outlet pickup. Gounley's coverage pattern shows that major real estate transactions, especially those involving iconic Denver properties or social impact angles, generate the broadest external interest.

**Justin Wingerter**

Wingerter achieved a solid 50% pickup rate with 2 of his 4 articles gaining external coverage, all coming from legal disputes in Denver's construction sector. His strongest piece on the construction firm owner seeking judicial closure of his "captive" company drew interest from both Hoodline and The Denver Post, while his coverage of Fading West's legal demands against a Granby developer also caught The Post's attention. 

Wingerter's beat clearly resonates with local media when focused on business litigation and corporate disputes, particularly those involving dramatic language or unusual legal remedies. Both picked-up stories centered on companies in financial or legal distress, suggesting his coverage of business conflicts and construction industry troubles generates the most external interest among Denver media outlets.

**Matt Geiger**

Geiger achieved perfect pickup coverage with all 4 articles gaining external attention, though primarily from a single outlet. The Denver Post consistently republished his real estate and development stories, covering his pieces on Northfield duplexes, Ramos Law's $3M office purchase, Luzzatto's $300M downtown project, and the golf-centric coworking expansion. His Ramos Law story generated the broadest interest with additional pickup on X.com.

Geiger's commercial real estate beat proves highly valuable to local media, with his focus on office purchases, development projects, and unique business concepts like golf coworking consistently attracting republication. The pattern suggests Denver outlets view his property transaction and development coverage as essential local business news, making him BusinessDen's most reliably syndicated reporter during this period.

**Max Scheinblum**

Scheinblum secured pickup on 3 of his 5 articles, with his restaurant and hospitality coverage drawing consistent external interest. His story on a former Avalanche defenseman launching a Denver beer brand and the Wash Park ice cream shop closure both attracted The Denver Post's attention, while his coverage of executive changes in restaurant groups found traction on X.com.

The pattern reveals that Scheinblum's food and beverage beat generates strong local media interest, particularly when stories involve recognizable Denver figures or neighborhood establishments. His most successful pickups blend local business news with cultural elements—sports celebrities entering business or beloved neighborhood spots closing—suggesting outlets value his coverage when it connects commerce to community identity.

**Sara B. Hansen**

Hansen's single article achieved 100% pickup rate, with The Denver Post republishing her coverage of February's top home sale—a $9M Country Club transaction. Though limited data, this suggests her luxury real estate coverage attracts consistent media attention when focused on high-value residential transactions in prestigious Denver neighborhoods.

**Thomas Gounley**

Gounley achieved strong performance with 4 of 6 articles picked up, demonstrating particular strength in high-impact real estate stories. His coverage of the Brown Palace sale generated the most significant external interest with 6 mentions across major outlets including 9News, Denver Gazette, and social media platforms, while his pieces on Broadway building sales and hotel transactions near Central Park also drew multiple pickups from The Denver Post and Hoodline.

Gounley's coverage pattern shows that iconic Denver properties and socially significant real estate developments generate the most external traction. His homeless services and affordable housing coverage consistently attracts pickup, suggesting local media views his reporting on the intersection of real estate and social issues as particularly newsworthy. The Brown Palace story's broad appeal across traditional media and social platforms highlights how his coverage of landmark Denver properties resonates beyond typical business readership.

I don't have access to the actual BusinessDen article or detailed reception data from the 2-day period following its March 2, 2026 publication. Based solely on the third-party mention provided, I can only note that The Denver Post picked up the story with a neutral framing, though the mention appears to be brief rather than substantial coverage.

To provide an accurate summary of the 2-day reception, I would need additional information about other outlets that covered the story and details about the extent and nature of that coverage.

# Summary of 2-Day Reception

BusinessDen's Brown Palace sale story received broad coverage across five outlets including major local news (9News, Denverite, Denver Gazette), social media (Instagram), and local press, with all mentions maintaining neutral framing. Coverage was primarily brief—mostly headlines and short mentions rather than substantial reporting—focusing on the basic fact that Crescent Real Estate had listed the iconic hotel and adjacent Holiday Inn Express for sale through Newmark. No outlets notably reframed the story differently; all presented it as straightforward real estate news without emphasizing BusinessDen's reporting on Crescent's "rocky ownership run."

# 2-Day Reception Summary

The BusinessDen article about Fentress's $13M Broadway property sale received pickup from two outlets within two days. The Denver Post ran a straightforward match of the original headline, while Hoodline reframed it more dramatically as a "Broadway Shakeup," though both maintained neutral coverage. Both mentions appear to be brief citations rather than substantial original reporting.

# 2-Day Reception Summary

The BusinessDen article about Luzzatto's $300M downtown project generated limited coverage, with only **The Denver Post** picking it up. The Denver Post's coverage appears to have been a **brief mention** rather than substantial reporting, as indicated by the truncated headline text. Both outlets maintained **neutral framing**, focusing on the ground lease approval as a procedural milestone in the office-to-residential conversion project without apparent editorial commentary or analysis differences.

# Summary of Article Reception

The BusinessDen article about Ramos Law's $3M southeast Denver office purchase received limited pickup over two days, with mentions from The Denver Post and X.com. Both outlets provided only brief mentions rather than substantial coverage, simply reprinting or referencing the core transaction details. The framing remained neutral across all sources, with no meaningful differences in how the story was presented.

# Summary

BusinessDen's article about a hotel and credit union sale near Central Park's homeless shelter received minimal pickup within 2 days. The Denver Post republished the story with identical framing, while a Facebook post provided a brief mention of the sale. Both outlets maintained neutral coverage without reframing the original reporting by Thomas Gounley.

# Summary

The BusinessDen article about the CEO succession at a restaurant group received minimal third-party coverage in its first two days. Only X (formerly Twitter) picked up the story, and only with a brief mention that simply repeated the headline without additional reporting or analysis. There were no notable framing differences, as the X post maintained a neutral tone identical to the original article's framing.

# Summary

The BusinessDen article about modular builder Fading West's legal demands against a Granby project developer received minimal third-party pickup within its first two days. Only The Denver Post picked up the story, running it as a brief mention with the same headline rather than substantial independent coverage. Both outlets maintained neutral framing of the dispute without adding interpretive commentary or additional reporting.
